french: APRES combat et bataille navalle,
Le grand Neptune à son plus haut besroy :
Rouge aversaire de peur viendra pasle,
Mettant le grand Ocean en effroy.
english: After combat and naval battle,
The great Neptune at his highest fury:
The red adversary will turn pale with fear,
Putting the great Ocean in terror.
1.interpretation: Following a major naval battle, the sea god Neptune reaches peak fury. A red-colored enemy (possibly symbolic of a nation or fleet) turns pale with terror, spreading fear across the vast ocean—evoking a decisive sea victory or a catastrophic naval event that instills widespread dread.

french: Le divin Verbe donra à la substance,
Comprins ciel, terre, or occult au laict mystique
Corps, ame, esprit ayant toute puissance,
Tant soubs ses pieds comme au siege Celique.
english: The divine Word will give to the substance,
Including sky, earth, gold hidden in the mystical milk,
Body, soul, spirit having all power,
As much under its feet as at the celestial seat.
2.interpretation: The divine Word infuses substance, encompassing heaven and earth along with occult gold in mystical form. Body, soul, and spirit gain complete dominion, reigning both on earth and in the heavenly throne—suggesting a profound spiritual or alchemical revelation that unifies material and divine realms.

french: Mars & Mercure, & l'argent joint ensemble,
Vers le Mydi extreme siccité :
Au fond d'Asie on dira terre tremble,
Corinthe, Ephese lors en perplexité.
english: Mars and Mercury, and silver joined together,
Toward the south extreme drought:
In the depths of Asia one will say the earth trembles,
Corinth, Ephesus then in perplexity.
3.interpretation: A planetary alignment involving Mars, Mercury, and the Moon (silver) brings severe drought in southern regions. Deep in Asia, earthquakes are reported, leaving the ancient cities of Corinth and Ephesus in confusion—indicating simultaneous climatic and seismic disruptions affecting both East and West.

french: Quand seront proche le defaut des lunaires,
De l'un a l'autre ne distant grandement,
Froid, siccité, dangers vers les frontières,
Mesme ou l'oracle a prins commencement.
english: When the defects of the lunaries are near,
From one to the other not greatly distant,
Cold, drought, dangers toward the frontiers,
Even where the oracle took its beginning.
4.interpretation: When lunar eclipses or irregularities approach closely (with little interval between them), cold, drought, and border dangers emerge. This occurs even at the site where an oracle originated—pointing to repeated lunar phenomena triggering harsh weather and threats in historically prophetic regions.

french: Pres loing defaut de deux grand luminaires,
Qui surviendra entre l'Avril & Mars :
O quel cherté : mais deux grands debonnaires
Par terre & mer secourront toutes parts.
english: Near and far defect of two great luminaries,
Which will occur between April and March:
Oh what dearness: but two great kind ones
By land and sea will aid all parts.
5.interpretation: A flaw or eclipse affecting two great celestial lights (Sun and Moon) appears near and far between March and April. Despite resulting scarcity and high prices, two benevolent powers will provide relief by both land and sea—suggesting astronomical events cause hardship, yet aid arrives from multiple directions.

french: Dans temples clos le foudre y entrera,
Les citadins dedans leur fort grevez :
Chevaux, boeufs, hommes, l'onde mur touchera
Par faim, soif, soubs les plus foibles armez.
english: In closed temples the lightning will enter,
The citizens inside their fort grieved:
Horses, oxen, men, the wave will touch the wall,
By hunger, thirst, under the weakest armed.
6.interpretation: Lightning strikes inside sealed temples. Citizens suffer while sheltered in their strongholds. Flood waters reach the walls, affecting people and livestock alike. Amid hunger and thirst, even the weakest take up arms—portraying a combined disaster of fire from heaven, flooding, and famine within a besieged or protected city.

french: Les fugitifs, feu du ciel sus les piques.
Conflict prochain des corbeaux s'esbatans,
De terre on crie, aide, secours celiques,
Quand pres des murs seront les combattants.
english: The fugitives, fire from the sky upon the pikes.
Next conflict of the ravens frolicking,
From the earth one cries, help, celestial succor,
When near the walls the combatants will be.
7.interpretation: Fleeing people face fire from the sky striking their spears or pikes. An imminent battle erupts, noisy like playful ravens. People on the ground cry out for heavenly aid as fighters close in on the walls—depicting desperate refugees, aerial attacks, and intense combat near fortifications.

french: Les Cimbres joints avecques leurs voisins,
Depopuler viendront presque l'Espaigne :
Gens amassés, Guienne & Limosins,
Seront en ligue, & leur feront compaigne.
english: The Cimbri joined with their neighbors,
Will come to almost depopulate Spain:
People gathered, Guienne and Limousins,
Will be in league, and will keep them company.
8.interpretation: The ancient Cimbri (or a northern people) allied with neighbors nearly empty Spain through invasion. Forces assembled from Guienne and Limousin form a league to confront them—suggesting a large-scale barbarian incursion into Iberia met by a southwestern French coalition.

french: Bourdeaux, Rouan, & la Rochelle joints,
Tiendront autour la grand mer Occeane,
Anglois, Bretons, & les Flamans conjoints,
Les chasseront jusqu'au pres de Rouane.
english: Bordeaux, Rouen, and La Rochelle joined,
Will hold around the great Ocean sea,
English, Bretons, and the Flemings joined,
Will chase them as far as near Rouen.
9.interpretation: The ports of Bordeaux, Rouen, and La Rochelle unite to control the Atlantic. Allied English, Breton, and Flemish forces drive their opponents back toward Rouen—indicating a naval and land campaign in which northern European allies push enemies deep into French territory.

french: De sang & faim plus grande calamité,
Sept fois s'appreste à la marine plage :
Monech de faim, lieu pris, capitvité,
Le grand mené croc enferrée cage.
english: Of blood and hunger a greater calamity,
Seven times prepares for the marine beach:
Monaco of hunger, place taken, captivity,
The great one led with a hook into an iron cage.
10.interpretation: Bloodshed and famine create an even greater disaster, striking the coastal area seven times. Starving Monaco is captured, leading to imprisonment. A great leader is hooked and dragged into an iron cage—foretelling repeated naval or coastal calamities culminating in the humiliating capture of a prominent figure.

french: Les armes battre au ciel longue saison,
L'arbre au milieu de la cité tombé :
Verbine, rogne, glaive, en face tyson,
Lors le Monarque d'Hadrie succombé.
english: The arms will beat the sky for a long season,
The tree in the middle of the city fallen:
Wormwood, scab, sword, in the face of a firebrand,
Then the Monarch of Hadria will succumb.
11.interpretation: Prolonged artillery fire or warfare will rage toward the sky. A central tree in the city falls, symbolizing collapse. Bitter suffering (wormwood), disease, sword, and fiery conflict ensue, culminating in the death of the ruler of the Adriatic region (Hadria)—pointing to a long war ending with the fall of a monarch amid widespread devastation.

french: Par la tumeur de Heb, Po, Tag, Timbre & Rome,
Et par l'estang Leman & Aretin :
Les deux grands chefs & citez de Garonne,
Prins, morts, noyez. Partir humain butin.
english: By the swelling of Heb, Po, Tag, Tiber and Rome,
And by the lake Leman and Aretine:
The two great chiefs and cities of the Garonne,
Taken, dead, drowned. Human booty to depart.
12.interpretation: Flooding or swelling of major rivers (Ebro, Po, Tagus, Tiber) and Rome, along with lakes Geneva and Aretine, will cause catastrophe. Two great leaders and the cities along the Garonne will be captured, killed, or drowned, with survivors taken as plunder—indicating devastating floods in Europe leading to loss of leadership and widespread displacement.

french: Par foudre en l'arche or & argent fondu,
De deux captifs l'un l'autre mangera :
De la cité le plus grand estendu,
Quand submergée la classe nagera.
english: By lightning in the ark gold and silver melted,
Of two captives one will eat the other:
Of the city the greatest extended,
When submerged the fleet will swim.
13.interpretation: Lightning strikes an ark-like structure, melting gold and silver. Two captives resort to cannibalism. The largest part of the city is devastated, and a submerged fleet struggles in the water—depicting a violent storm or disaster causing economic ruin, desperate survival, and naval losses.

french: Par le rameau du vaillant personnage,
De France infime, par le père infelice :
Honneurs, richesses, travail en son vieil âge,
Pour avoir creu le conseil d'homme nice.
english: By the branch of the valiant personage,
Of lowly France, by the unhappy father:
Honours, riches, toil in his old age,
For having believed the counsel of a foolish man.
14.interpretation: Through the lineage of a brave but minor French figure, born of an unfortunate father, honours and wealth arrive only late in life accompanied by hardship—because he trusted the advice of a naive or foolish man. This suggests a delayed rise marked by poor judgment and personal struggle.

french: Coeur, vigueur, gloire le regne changera,
De tous points contre ayant son adversaire:
Lors France enfance par mort subjuguera,
Un grand Regent sera lors plus contraire.
english: Heart, vigor, glory the reign will change,
From all sides having his adversary against him:
Then France childhood by death will subjugate,
A great Regent will then be more contrary.
15.interpretation: A ruler full of heart, vigor, and glory will transform the kingdom while facing enemies on every side. France’s young generation will be crushed by death, and a powerful regent will prove even more hostile—foretelling a dynamic but brutal reign that devastates the youth and intensifies opposition.

french: Un Prince Anglois Mars à son coeur de ciel,
Voudra poursuivre sa fortune prospere :
Des deux duelles l'un percera le siel,
Hay de lui, bien aimé de sa mère.
english: An English Prince Mars in his heart of heaven,
Will want to pursue his prosperous fortune:
Of the two duels one will pierce the sky,
Hated by him, well loved by his mother.
16.interpretation: An English prince, martial by nature and ambitious (“Mars in his heart of heaven”), will chase great success. In two duels, one will strike dramatically. He will be hated by one party but deeply loved by his mother—suggesting a bold royal figure whose conflicts and personal life shape his destiny.

french: Mont Aventine brusler nuict sera veu,
Le ciel obscur tout à un coup en Flandres,
Quand le Monarque chassera son neveu,
Leurs gens d'Eglise commettront les esclandres.
english: Mount Aventine burning at night will be seen,
The sky obscure all at once in Flanders,
When the Monarch will chase his nephew,
Their churchmen will commit the scandals.
17.interpretation: A fire on Rome’s Mount Aventine will be visible at night, coinciding with sudden darkness over Flanders. As a monarch pursues or banishes his nephew, religious figures will cause scandals—linking dynastic strife, ominous celestial signs, and clerical misconduct.

french: Apres la pluie laict assez longuette,
En plusieurs lieux de Rheims le ciel touché :
O quel conflict de sang pres d'eux s'appreste,
Pere & fils Roys n'oseront approché.
english: After the rain milk quite longish,
In several places of Rheims the sky touched:
Oh what a conflict of blood near them prepares,
Father and son kings will not dare approach.
18.interpretation: Following prolonged rainfall, a milky substance (possibly hail, foam, or atmospheric phenomenon) lingers. In the region of Rheims the sky is struck in multiple places. A bloody conflict brews nearby, causing even royal father and son to avoid the area—suggesting ominous weather signs preceding violent strife.

french: En Luques sang & laict viendra plouvoir,
Un peu devant changement de preteur :
Grand peste & guerre, faim & soif fera voir,
Loin ou mourra leur Prince recteur.
english: In Lucca blood and milk will come to rain,
A little before the change of praetor:
Great plague and war, hunger and thirst will make seen,
Far away where their prince rector will die.
19.interpretation: In Lucca, a rain of blood and milk (prodigious sign) will fall shortly before a change in leadership. Massive plague, war, famine, and thirst will strike, and the ruling prince will die far from home—foretelling disaster and political transition accompanied by the distant death of a leader.

french: Par les contrées du grand fleuve Bethique,
Loin d'Ibere au royaume de Grenade :
Croix repoussées par gens Mahometiques,
Un de Cordube trahira la contrade.
english: Through the lands of the great Betic river,
Far from Iberia in the kingdom of Granada:
Crosses repelled by Mahometan peoples,
One from Cordoba will betray the country.
20.interpretation: Along the Guadalquivir (Betic) river in the kingdom of Granada, Christian crosses will be driven back by Muslim forces. A man from Cordoba will betray the region—indicating religious conflict and internal treason in Moorish Spain.

french: Au Crustamin par mer Hadriatique,
Apparoistra un horrible poisson,
De face humaine, & la fin aquatique,
Qui se prendra dehors de l'ameçon.
english: At the Crustamin by the Adriatic sea,
A horrible fish will appear,
With human face, and the aquatic end,
Which will be taken outside the hook.
21.interpretation: Off the Adriatic coast (possibly near a specific shore or “Crustamin”), a monstrous fish with a human-like face will surface, leading to a watery fate for those not safely hooked—evoking a terrifying sea omen or creature causing maritime peril.

french: Six jours l'assaut devant cité donné :
Livrée sera forte & aspre bataille :
Trois la rendront, & à eux pardonné,
Le reste à feu & sang tranche taille.
english: Six days the assault before the city given:
A strong and harsh battle will be delivered:
Three will surrender it, and to them pardoned,
The rest with fire and blood cut to pieces.
22.interpretation: A six-day assault precedes the fall of a city. A fierce battle ensues; three parties surrender and receive pardon, while the remaining defenders are slaughtered by fire and sword—describing a relatively short but brutal siege with selective mercy.

french: Si France passe outre mer lygustique,
Tu te verras en isles & mers enclos :
Mahommet contraire, plus mer Hadriatique,
Chevaux & Asnes tu rongeras les os.
english: If France passes beyond the Ligurian sea,
You will see yourself in islands and enclosed seas:
Muhammad opposite, more Adriatic sea,
Horses and donkeys you will gnaw the bones.
23.interpretation: Should France venture beyond the Ligurian Sea (western Mediterranean), it will become trapped among islands and confined waters. Opposing Muslim forces from the Adriatic will threaten, leaving horses and donkeys to be devoured in desperation—warning of a risky naval expedition ending in entrapment and famine.

french: De l'entreprinse grande confusion.
Perte de gens, thrésor innumerable :
Tu n'y dois faire encore tension,
France a mon dire fais que sois recordable.
english: From the enterprise great confusion.
Loss of people, treasure innumerable:
You must not make further tension there,
France, to my saying make yourself memorable.
24.interpretation: A grand undertaking will result in chaos, enormous loss of life, and vast treasure. France should not push further; instead, heed the warning and create a lasting legacy—advising restraint after a costly and confusing venture.

french: Qui au Royaume Navarrois parviendra,
Quand de Sicile & Naples seront joints :
Bigore & Landes par Foix loron tiendra,
D'un qui d'Espagne sera par trop conjoinct.
english: Who in the Navarrese kingdom will attain,
When Sicily and Naples will be joined:
Bigorre and Landes by Foix Loron will hold,
From one who from Spain will be too closely joined.
25.interpretation: A figure will rise to power in the kingdom of Navarre when Sicily and Naples unite. The regions of Bigorre and Landes will be controlled by Foix Loron (a regional power), linked to someone overly allied with Spain—suggesting political realignment through marriage or alliance in southern France and Spain.

french: Des Roys & Princes dresseront simulacres,
Augures, creuz eslevez aruspices :
Corne victime d'orée, & d'azur, d'acre,
Interpretez seront les extipices.
english: Kings and Princes will raise simulacra,
Auguries, raised crosses, haruspices:
Horned victim gilded, and of azure, of acrid,
The entrails will be interpreted.
26.interpretation: Rulers will erect false images or idols. Omens, elevated crosses, and diviners (haruspices) will proliferate. Sacrificial victims with gilded horns and colored offerings will have their entrails examined for prophecies—indicating a revival of pagan or elaborate divinatory rituals under royal patronage.

french: Prince Libinique puissant en Occident,
Francois d'Arabe viendra tant enflammer,
Scavant aux lettres fera condescendent,
La langue Arabe en Francois translater.
english: Libyan Prince powerful in the Occident,
Francis of Arabia will come to inflame so much,
Knowing in letters will make condescending,
The Arabic language into French translate.
27.interpretation: A powerful Libyan prince will exert influence in the West. A figure named Francis of Arabia will passionately stir events. Scholars will humbly engage with texts, leading to the translation of Arabic works into French—pointing to significant cultural and intellectual exchange between the Arab world and France.

french: De terre foible & pauvre parentèle,
Par bout & paix parviendra dans l'Empire,
Long temps regner une jeune femelle,
Quonques en regne n'en survint un si pire.
english: From weak land and poor parentage,
By end and peace will attain the Empire,
For a long time a young female will reign,
Never in the reign did a worse one appear.
28.interpretation: From a humble and weak background, a figure will reach the empire through the conclusion of conflict and peace. A young woman will rule for many years, yet her reign will prove exceptionally bad—suggesting the unexpected rise of a female monarch whose long rule brings significant misfortune.

french: Les deux nepueus en divers lieux nourris:
Nauale pugne, terre, pères tombés
Viendront si haut esleués enguerris
Venger l’iniure: ennemis succombés.
english: The two nephews in different places fed:
Naval fight, earth, fathers fallen
Will come so high raised enemies
To avenge the injury: enemies defeated.
29.interpretation: Two nephews, each nourished in separate locations, will lead naval battles amid fallen patriarchs. They will rise high, defeating enemies to avenge past wounds—indicating a family‑driven war of revenge across sea and land.

french: Celuy qu’en luite & fer au fait bellique,
Aura porté plus grand que lui le pris,
De nuit au lit six lui feront la pique,
Nud sans harnois subit sera surpris.
english: He who in light and iron at warlike deed,
Will bear greater than him the price,
At night on the bed six will make the sting,
Naked without armor will suddenly be surprised.
30.interpretation: A warrior renowned for bright, iron warfighting will pay a higher price than expected. Six assassins will strike him at night while he is unarmed. This foretells a surprise attack on a famed combatant.

french: Aux champs de Mede, d’Arabe & d’Armenie,
Deux grands copies trois fois s’assembleront:
Pres du riuage d’Araxes la mesnie,
Du grand Solman en terre tomberont.
english: In the fields of Mede, Arab and Armenian,
Two great copies three times will assemble:
Near the flooding of Araxes the mess,
From the great Solomon will fall to earth.
31.interpretation: In regions of Media, Arab lands and Armenia, three powerful forces (copies) will gather. Near the Araxes river’s flood, a major figure (Solomon) will fall, indicating a coalition of eastern powers and a catastrophic defeat of a great ruler.

french: Le grand sepulcre du peuple Aquitanique
S’approchera auprès de la Tousquane,
Quand Mars sera près du coing Germanique,
Et au terroir de la gent Mantuane.
english: The great tomb of the Aquitanian people
Will approach near the Tousquane,
When Mars is near the Germanic corner,
And to the land of the Mantuan people.
32.interpretation: A massive burial (catastrophe) affecting Aquitaine will reach near the region of Tousquane. At the same time Mars (war) will be close to Germanic borders, and the Mantuan lands will also be affected—signalling widespread war and death across western Europe.

french: En la cité où le loup entrera,
Bien près de là les ennemis seront:
Copie étrange grand païs gastera.
Aux murs & Alpes les amis passeront.
english: In the city where the wolf will enter,
Very near there the enemies will be:
Strange copy great country will be destroyed.
To the walls and Alps the friends will pass.
33.interpretation: A city breached by a wolf (symbolic of invasion) will soon be surrounded by foes. A foreign “copy” (perhaps a replica army) will devastate a great land, while allies will move toward the walls and the Alps—indicating a siege with both destructive invaders and relieving forces.

french: Quand le defaut du soleil lors sera,
Sus le plain jour le monstre sera vu:
Tout autrement on l’interpretera.
Cherté n’a garde: nul ny aura pourue.
english: When the sun’s defect will be then,
Above the plain day the monster will be seen:
Everything else will be interpreted.
Cost has no guard: none will have it ruined.
34.interpretation: A sun defect (eclipse) will reveal a monster in the daylight plains. All other signs will be re‑interpreted, and wealth (cost) will no longer protect anyone—foretelling a solar eclipse that unveils a terrifying omen and nullifies previous safeguards.

french: Du plus profond de l’Occident d’Europe,
De paures gens vn jeune enfant naistra,
Qui par sa langue séduira grande troupe:
Son bruit au regne d’Orient plus croistra.
english: From the deepest West of Europe,
From poor people a young child will be born,
Who by his language will seduce a great crowd:
His fame in the Eastern reign will increase more.
35.interpretation: In the far western part of Europe a child of humble origin will be born. His speech will attract large followings, and his reputation will grow especially in the East—suggesting a charismatic figure from the West who will influence Eastern powers.

french: Enseueli non mort apopletique
Sera trouue auvoir les mains mangées:
Quand la cité damnera l’heretique,
Qu’auoit leurs loix si leur sembloit changées.
english: Enseueli not dead apoplectic
Will be found having eaten hands:
When the city will condemn the heretic,
That their laws seemed changed.
36.interpretation: A person named Enseueli, not dead but maddened, will be discovered having mutilated himself. The city will denounce a heretic, altering its laws in the process—suggesting a violent madness that spurs religious persecution and legal reform.

french: Auant l’assaut oraison prononcée:
Milan prins d’aigle par embusches deceuz:
Muraille antique par canons enfoncée,
Par feu & sang à mercy peu receuz.
english: Before the assault prayer spoken:
Milan prince of eagle by ambushes deceived:
Ancient wall broken by cannons,
By fire & blood little mercy received.
37.interpretation: A prayer will precede an attack. The Milanese ruler (eagle symbol) will be outwitted by ambushes. An old fortification will be shattered by artillery, with fire and blood offering little mercy—foretelling a brutal siege of Milan.

french: La gent Gauloise & nation étrangère
Outre les monts, morts prins & profligés:
Au mois contraire & proche de vendange
Par les seigneurs en accord rédigés.
english: The Gaulish people and foreign nation
Beyond the mountains, dead princes and plundered:
In the opposite month and near harvest
By the lords in agreement written.
38.interpretation: Gauls and foreign peoples beyond the mountains will see princes killed and loot taken. During an off‑season close to harvest, lords will formalise treaties—suggesting a delayed yet decisive pact after heavy losses.

french: Les sept en trois mis en concorde
Pour subiuguer des alpes Apennines:
Mais la tempeste & Ligure couarde
Les profligent en subites ruines.
english: The seven in three placed in agreement,
To subjugate the Alpine Apennines:
But the storm and cowardly Ligurian
Will plunge them into sudden ruins.
39.interpretation: Seven forces, grouped as three alliances, will try to dominate the Alpine‑Apennine region. However, a storm and a timid Ligurian side will cause their rapid downfall—signalling an alliance’s collapse due to natural and political weakness.

french: Le grand theatre se viendra redresser:
Le dez geté, & les rets ia tendus.
Trop le premier en glaz viendra lasser,
Par arcs prostraits de long temps ia fendus.
english: The great theatre will come to be restored:
The dice cast, and the nets are stretched.
Too much the first in ice will come to tire,
By bows prostrated for a long time they were split.
40.interpretation: A major cultural center (the theatre) will be rebuilt; gambling (dice) and fishing nets will be set. Excessive ice will cause fatigue, and long‑standing bows (perhaps weapons) will break—implying a revival of arts amid harsh winter conditions that damage armaments.

french: Bosseu sera esleu par le conseil,
Plus hideux monstre en terre n’aperceu.
Le coup volant prelat creuera l’œil:
Le traistre au roy pour fidele receu.
english: Bosseu will be chosen by the council,
No more hideous monster seen on earth.
The flying blow of the prelate will create the eye:
The traitor to the king for faithful receives.
41.interpretation: A figure named Bosseu will be appointed by a council, ending the sight of a terrifying monster. A prelate’s swift strike will expose the traitor, who will be rewarded for loyalty to the king—suggesting a political purge and reward system.

french: L’enfant naistra à deux dents à la gorge
Pierres en Tuscie par pluie tomberont:
Peu d’ans apres ne sera bled, ne orge,
Pour saouler ceux qui de faim failliront.
english: The child will be born with two teeth in the throat
Stones in Tuscia will fall by rain:
Few years later it will not be wheat, nor barley,
To drown those who will fail from hunger.
42.interpretation: A child born with a malformation (two teeth in the throat) heralds a disaster where stones rain down in Tuscia. Within years, crops will fail, leading to famine and drowning of those who cannot eat—foretelling a famine accompanied by a catastrophic storm.

french: Gents d’alentour de Tarn, Loth, & Garonne,
Gardés les monts Apennines passer,
Vostre tombeau pres de Rome & d'Anconne
Le noir poil crespe fera trophée dresser.
english: People around Tarn, Loth, & Garonne,
Guard the Apennine mountains to pass,
Your tomb near Rome & Ancona
The black curly hair will raise a trophy.
43.interpretation: Inhabitants of the Tarn, Loth and Garonne regions will defend the Apennines. A burial site near Rome and Ancona will be marked by a trophy fashioned from dark curly hair—perhaps a symbol of a defeated enemy—signalling a defensive stand in Italy and a commemorative monument.

french: Quand l’animal à l’homme domestique
Apres grands peines & saults viendra parler:
Le foudre à vierge sera si maleficque,
De terre prinse, & suspendue en l’air.
english: When the animal to the domestic man
After great pains and jumps will come to speak:
The lightning to the virgin will be so malignant,
From the earth taken, and suspended in the air.
44.interpretation: A domesticated animal (perhaps a horse) will, after suffering, begin to ‘speak’—a metaphor for a warning. A deadly lightning strike will hit a virgin, lifting something from the ground into the sky—suggesting a supernatural omen involving a pure figure and a storm.

french: Les cinq étranges entrés dedans le temple,
Leur sang viendra la terre prophaner:
Aux Thoulousains sera bien dur exemple
D’un qui viendra ses loÿs exterminer.
english: The five strange ones entered within the temple,
Their blood will make the earth barren:
To the Thoulous will be a hard example
Of one who will come to exterminate his laws.
45.interpretation: Five mysterious figures will infiltrate a temple, spilling blood that renders the land barren. The people of Thoulous will receive a harsh lesson from a person who will eradicate his own laws—hinting at a sacrilegious act that devastates a region and leads to legal destruction.

french: Le ciel (de Plancus la cité) nous presaige
Par clairs insignes & par estoiles fixes,
Que de son change subit s’approche l’âge,
Ne pour son bien, ne pour ses maléfices.
english: The sky (of Plancus the city) will seize us
By clear signs and by fixed stars,
From its sudden change the age approaches,
Neither for its good, nor for its evils.
46.interpretation: A celestial event over Plancus (a city) will strike, marked by clear omens and steady stars. This abrupt shift signals a new era, neither wholly beneficial nor wholly harmful—foretelling a pivotal epochal change driven by astronomical signs.

french: Le vieux monarque deschassé de son règne
Aux Orient[s] son secours ira querre:
Pour peur des croix pliera son enseigne:
En Mitilène ira pour port et terre.
english: The old monarch expelled from his reign
To the East his aid will go to seek:
For fear of the cross he will bend his banner:
In Miletus he will go for port and land.
47.interpretation: An ousted elderly king will look eastward for assistance. Fearing the Christian cross, he will change his standard and travel to Miletus (Asia Minor) seeking a harbor and territory—signalling a displaced ruler turning to the East for refuge and power.

french: Sept cents captifs estaches rudement
Pour la moitié meurtrir, donné le sort,
Le proche espoir viendra si promptement
Mais non si tost qu’une quinzième mort.
english: Seven hundred captives harshly torn
For half to be killed, fate given,
Near hope will come so quickly,
But not as soon as a fifteenth death.
48.interpretation: Seven hundred prisoners will suffer brutal fates, with half dying. A glimmer of hope will arrive relatively fast, though not as fast as the next death among a group of fifteen—suggesting a grim captivity with a delayed but eventual rescue.

french: Regne Gauloys tu seras bien change:
En lieu étranger est translaté l’empire
Entre autres morts, & loÿs seras rangé :
Rouan & Chartres te feront bien du pire.
english: The Gaulish reign you will be well changed:
In a foreign place the empire is transferred
Among other deaths, and laws you will be arranged:
Rouen & Chartres will do you great evil.
49.interpretation: The Gaulish kingdom will undergo a profound transformation, moving its empire to a foreign land. Many will die and laws will be reordered. The cities of Rouen and Chartres will bring about severe misfortune—signalling a forced relocation and legal upheaval with hostile French cities.

french: La république de la grande cité
A grand rigueur ne voudra consentir:
Roi sortir hors par trompette cité
L’eschele au mur, la cité repentir.
english: The republic of the great city
Will not want to consent to great rigor:
King will exit out by trumpet of the city
The ladder to the wall, the city repent.
50.interpretation: A major urban republic will refuse harsh discipline. A monarch will depart, signalled by a city’s trumpet. A ladder placed against a wall symbolizes the city’s remorse—foretelling a sovereign’s forced exit and an embarrassed civic authority.

french: P A R I S coniure vn grand meurtre commetre,
Bloys le fera sortir en plain effet:
Ceux d’Orleans voudront leur chef remetre,
Angiers, Troye, Langres leur feront grand forfait.
english: Paris conspires to commit a great murder,
Blois will bring it about in full effect,
Those of Orléans will want to restore their leader,
Angers, Troyes, Langres will give them a heavy penalty.
51.interpretation: A political plot in Paris will lead to a high‑profile assassination. Blois will be the instrument, while the people of Orléans demand the return of their chief. The cities of Angers, Troyes and Langres will retaliate harshly, signalling civil strife in central France.

french: En la Campaigne sera si longue pluie,
Et en la Pouile si grande siccité.
Coq verra l’aigle, l’aesle mal accomplie:
Par Lyon mise sera en extremité.
english: In the countryside a very long rain will fall,
And in Pouile an extreme drought,
The rooster will see the eagle, the task ill‑done:
Through Lyon a final thrust will be placed.
52.interpretation: A region will experience prolonged rain, while another suffers severe drought. A symbolic clash (rooster vs. eagle) will end badly, and Lyon will become the decisive battleground, indicating contrasting weather disasters leading to conflict in the region.

french: Quand le plus grand emportera le pris
De Nuremberg d’Auspurg, & ceux de Basle
Par Aggripine chef Francqfort repris
Transuerseront par Flamans iusques en Gale.
english: When the greatest takes the prize
From Nuremberg, Augsburg and those of Basel,
By the aggressive French‑Frankfurt leader taken back,
They will be driven across by the Flemish into Gaul.
53.interpretation: A dominant power will capture the spoils of German cities (Nuremberg, Augsburg, Basel). A force led from Frankfurt will reclaim them, only to be forced back by the Flemish into French lands—forecasting a clash over the Holy Roman Empire’s western territories.

french: L’un des plus grands fuira aux Hespaignes,
Qu’en longue plaie apres viendra saigner:
Passant copies par les hautes montaignes
Deuastant tout & puis en paix regner.
english: One of the greatest will flee to Spain,
A long wound will later bleed,
Copies will pass over the high mountains,
Ravaging all and then ruling in peace.
54.interpretation: A prominent figure will escape to Spain, leaving a lingering grievance that later erupts. Troops (copies) will cross the high mountains, devastate the region, and subsequently establish a peaceful rule—suggesting an invasion of Spain followed by a harsh but stabilising occupation.

french: En l’an qu’un oeil en France regnera,
La court sera à vn bien fascheux trouble:
Le grand de Bloys son ami tuera:
Le regne mis en mal & doute double.
english: In the year an eye will rule in France,
The court will be in a very wicked trouble,
The great of Blois will kill his friend,
The reign will be placed in difficulty and double doubt.
55.interpretation: A ruler (symbolised by an eye) will ascend in France, bringing corruption at court. The leader of Blois will betray and murder an ally, causing the monarchy to fall into crisis and deep uncertainty.

french: Montauban, Nismes, Auignon, & Besier,
Peste, tonnerre & gresle à fin de Mars:
De Paris pont, Lyon mur, Montpellier,
Depuis six cent & sept XXIII.pars.
english: Montauban, Nîmes, Auvergne, & Besier,
Plague, thunder and hail at the end of March,
From the Paris bridge, Lyon wall, Montpellier,
Since six hundred and seven twenty‑three pars.
56.interpretation: Mid‑March will bring plague, storms and hail affecting several French cities, while bridges and walls in Paris, Lyon and Montpellier are threatened—signalling a widespread natural disaster across southern France.

french: Sept foys changer verrés gent Britannique
Taintz en sang en deux cent nonante an:
Franche non point par apui Germanique.
Aries doute son pole Bastarnan.
english: Seven times the British people will be changed,
Stained with blood in the year two‑hundred ninety,
France will not be supported by the Germanic.
Aries doubts its pole Bastarnan.
57.interpretation: In the year 290, Britain will suffer repeated upheavals and bloodshed. France will lack Germanic support, and Aries (the zodiac sign) will question its own destiny—indicating political turmoil in Britain and isolation for France.

french: Aupres du Rin des montaignes Noriques
Naistra vn grand de gents trop tard venu,
Qui defendra SAVROME & Pannoniques,
Qu’on ne saura qu’il sera deuenu.
english: Near the Rhine of the Noric mountains,
A great man of people will be born too late,
Who will defend Savrome and Pannonian lands,
It will not be known what he will become.
58.interpretation: A late‑born leader from the Noric (Austrian) region near the Rhine will try to protect the Savoy and Pannonian territories, but his fate remains uncertain—foretelling a delayed heroic figure whose impact is unclear.

french: Barbare empire par le tiers vsurpé
La plus grand part de son sang metra à mort:
Par mort senile par luy le quart frapé,
Pour peur que sang par le sang ne soit mort.
english: Barbarian empire overrun by a third,
The greatest part of its blood will be put to death:
By senile death a quarter will be struck,
For fear that blood by blood will not die.
59.interpretation: A barbarian empire will be split, with most of its warriors slain. A quarter will die of old age, out of fear of endless bloodshed—signalling internal collapse and a tragic loss of life.

french: Par toute Asie grande proscription,
Mesmes en Mysie, Lysie & Pamphylie:
Sang versera par absolution
D’un jeune noir rempli de félonie.
english: Throughout all great Asia a ban,
Also in Mysia, Lycia and Pamphylia:
Blood will be shed by absolution
Of a young black man full of treachery.
60.interpretation: A sweeping exile will affect much of Asia and parts of western Turkey. A treacherous young man will cause bloodshed under the guise of pardoning—suggesting a major political purge across the eastern Mediterranean.

french: La grande bande & secte crucigere
Se dressera en Mesopotamie:
Du proche fleuue compaignie legiere,
Que telle loi tiendra pour ennemie.
english: A great gang and crusader sect
Will arise in Mesopotamia:
From the nearby river a light company,
That such law will hold for the enemy.
61.interpretation: A militant crusading group will emerge in Mesopotamia, supported by a fleet on a nearby river. Its legal code will target foes, indicating a new religious‑military power in the Middle East.

french: Proche del duero par mer Tyrrene close
Viendra percer les grands monts Pyrénées.
La main plus courte & sa percée gloze,
A Carcassonne conduira ses menées.
english: Near the Duero close to the Tyrrhenian sea,
Will pierce the great Pyrenean mountains.
The short hand and its shining breach,
Will lead its forces to Carcassonne.
62.interpretation: A force from the Duero River region (Spain) will break through the Pyrenees, advancing toward Carcassonne in France—signalling a western invasion over the mountain barrier.

french: Romain pouuoir sera du tout abas,
Son grand voysin imiter ses vestiges:
Occultes haines ciuiles, & debats
Retarderont aux bouffons leurs folligges.
english: Roman power will be completely destroyed,
Its great neighbor will imitate its ruins:
Hidden urban hatreds, and debates
Will delay the clowns in their follies.
63.interpretation: The Roman Empire will collapse, with a successor state copying its remains. Internal urban hostilities and endless debates will stall foolish leaders—foretelling the fall of Rome and the rise of a mimic power.

french: Le chef de Perse remplira grande OLXA​ES
Classe trireme contre gent Mahumetique
De Parthe, & Mede: piller les Cyclades:
Long repos au grand port Ionique.
english: The Persian chief will fill a great fleet,
A trireme class against the Muslim peoples
From Persia and Media to pillage the Cyclades:
Long rest at the great Ionian port.
64.interpretation: Persia will assemble a powerful navy (triremes) to fight Muslim forces, raiding the Aegean islands before returning to a safe Ionian harbor—a projection of Persian maritime power into the Mediterranean.

french: Quand le sepulcre du grand Romain trouué,
Le iour apres sera esleu pontife,
Du senat gueres il ne sera prouué
Empoisonné son sang au sacré scyphe.
english: When the tomb of the great Roman is found,
The following day a pope will be chosen,
From the senate he will hardly be proven,
His blood poisoned by the sacred scepter.
65.interpretation: The discovery of a major Roman tomb will coincidingly lead to the election of a pope whose legitimacy is doubtful; he will be poisoned by a sacred object—foretelling controversy in the Papacy linked to ancient Roman findings.

french: Le grand bailif d’Orleans mis à mort
Sera par vn de sang vindicatif:
De mort merite ne mourra, ne par sort:
Des pieds & mains mal le faisoit captif.
english: The great bailiff of Orléans will be killed,
By a vindictive blood relative:
He will not die by merit, nor by fate:
His feet and hands made him a poor captive.
66.interpretation: Orléans’ chief magistrate will be murdered by a vengeful family member, not for justice. He will be captured and tortured—foretelling an internal vendetta that ends in a brutal death of a high official.

french: Vne nouvelle secte de Philosophes
Mesprisant mort, or, honneurs & richesses,
Des monts Germains ne seront limitrophes:
A les ensuiure auront apui & presses.
english: A new sect of Philosophers
Despising death, gold, honors and riches,
The Germanic mountains will not be their borders:
They will have support and presses behind them.
67.interpretation: A philosophical movement will reject material concerns, spreading beyond the German mountains, supported by patrons and presses—signifying an intellectual uprising that transcends regional limits.

french: Peuple sans chef d’Espagne & d’Italie
Morts, profligés dans le Cherronnesse:
Leur duyt trahi par légère folie
Le sang nager par tout à la trauerse.
english: People without a leader in Spain and Italy
Dead, wasteful in the Corrèze region:
Their leaders betrayed by a light folly
Blood will flow everywhere in the crossing.
68.interpretation: Leaderless populations in Spain and Italy will suffer massive casualties in Corrèze (France) due to a trivial betrayal, leading to widespread bloodshed—indicating a misguided alliance causing heavy losses.

french: Grand exercite conduict par iouuenceau,
Se viendra rendre aux mains des ennemis:
Mais le viellard nay au demi pourceau,
Fera Chalon & Mascon être amis.
english: Great army led by a youth,
Will be handed over to the enemies' hands:
But the old man will not be a half‑pig,
Will make Chalon and Mascon friends.
69.interpretation: A youthful commander will lose his army to the enemy. An aged figure, however, will broker an alliance between Chalon and Mascon—signalling a reversal of fortunes through unexpected diplomacy.

french: La grand Bretagne comprinse l’Angleterre
Viendra par eaux si hault à inunder
La ligue neusue d’Ausonne fera guerre,
Que contre eux mêmes il se viendront bander.
english: Great Britain will comprehend England,
Will come by waters so high to invade,
The new league of Auvergne will wage war,
That they will bind themselves against each other.
70.interpretation: Britain will assert dominance over England via a naval invasion. Meanwhile, a new coalition from Auvergne will go to war, but internal conflicts will cause them to turn on themselves—foretelling a British incursion and a fracturing French league.

french: Ceux dans les isles de long temps assiégés
Prendront vigueur force contre ennemis:
Ceux par dehors morts de faim profligés,
En plus grand faim que jamais seront mis.
english: Those on islands long besieged
Will gain strength against enemies:
Those outside, dead from famine, scattered,
Will be placed in greater hunger than ever.
71.interpretation: Island populations under a prolonged siege will become more resilient, while those on the mainland die of famine, leading to unprecedented hunger—suggesting a split fate between besieged islands and starving mainland.

french: Le bon viellard tout vif enseueli,
Pres du grand fleuue par fauce souspeçon:
Le nouueau vieux de richesse ennobli
Prins au chemin tout l’or de la rançon.
english: The good old man, very lively, will be summoned,
Near the great river by false suspicion:
The new elderly of noble wealth
Will take on the road all the gold of ransom.
72.interpretation: An energetic elder will be called to action near a major river under false accusations. A newly ennobled wealthy elder will profit from ransom payments—foretelling a scandal involving a respected elder and a rich, opportunistic figure.

french: Quand dans le regne paruiendra le boiteux
Competiteur aura proche bastard:
Luy & le regne viendront si fort rogneux,
Qu’ains qu’il guerisse son fait sera bien tard.
english: When the lame one appears in the reign,
A competitor will have a near‑born bastard:
He and the reign will become very angry,
Even if he heals, his deed will be very late.
73.interpretation: A physically impaired ruler will see a rival with an illegitimate heir. Their anger will flare, and even recovery will come too late—signalling a dynastic crisis fueled by disability and illegitimacy.

french: Naples, Florence, Fauence & Imole,
Seront en termes de telle facherie,
Que pour complaire aux malheureux de Nolle,
Plainct d’auvoir fait à son chef moquerie.
english: Naples, Florence, Fauence and Imole,
Will be in such quarrels,
To please the unhappy of Nolle,
Complaint of having mocked their chief.
74.interpretation: Italian city‑states will quarrel to appease disgruntled parties from Nolle, ending up mocking their own leader—indicating internal Italian conflicts and humiliation of authority.

french: P A V. Veronne, Vicence, Sarragousse
De glaïfues loin terroirs de sang humides:
Peste si grande viendra à la grand gousse
Proches secours, & bien loing les remèdes.
english: P A V. Veronne, Vicence, Sarragousse
From distant wet blooded lands:
A great plague will come to the great husk
Near help, and far the remedies.
75.interpretation: Regions such as Veronne, Vicence and Sarragossa will be struck by a massive plague; aid will be nearby but effective cures will be distant—foretelling an epidemic with limited immediate relief.

french: En Germanie naistront diverses sectes,
S’approcheront fort de l’heureux paganisme,
Le cœur captif & petites réceptes,
Feront retour à payer le vrai diſme.
english: In Germany various sects will be born,
They will approach closely the happy paganism,
The captive heart and small receptions,
Will return to pay the true tax.
76.interpretation: New cults in Germany will move toward pagan practices. Their hidden believers will eventually have to pay the proper tribute (perhaps to the state or church), signifying religious diversification and a later re‑imposition of authority.

french: Le tiers climat sous Aries comprins
L'an mil sept cens vingt et sept en Octobre
Le Roy de Perse par ceux d’Egypte prins :
Conflit, mort perte : à la croix grand opprobre.
english: The third climate comprised under Aries,
The year one thousand seven hundred twenty-seven in October,
The King of Persia captured by those of Egypt:
Conflict, death, loss: great opprobrium to the cross.
77.interpretation: This quatrain predicts that in October 1727, under the astrological influence of Aries in the 'third climate' (likely denoting the eastern or Middle Eastern regions), the King of Persia will be captured by Egyptian forces, unleashing conflict, death, and losses that bring profound shame to Christianity (the cross). No such event transpired in 1727 amid the Ottoman-Persian War, prompting scholars to interpret the date symbolically, often linking it to prospective Iran-Egypt hostilities with global religious ramifications.

french: Le chef d’Escosse avec six d’Alemagne
Par gens de mer Orientaux captifs,
Transueront le Calpe & Espagne
Présent en Perse au nouveau roy craintif.
english: The chief of Scotland with six of Germany
By captive eastern sea peoples,
Will traverse the Calpe and Spain
Present in Persia to the new fearful king.
78.interpretation: A Scottish leader allied with six German forces will be captured by eastern maritime peoples. They will cross the Strait of Gibraltar into Spain, then appear in Persia before a new, fearful ruler—foretelling a complex north‑south‑east entanglement.

french: L’ordre fatal sempiternel par chaîne
Viendra tourner par ordre conséquent:
Du port Phocen sera rompue la chaîne:
La cité prinse, l’ennemi quand & quand.
english: The eternal fatal order by chain
Will turn by consequent order:
From the Phocian port the chain will be broken:
The city taken, the enemy when and when.
79.interpretation: A perpetual destiny enforced by chains will shift according to events. The chain at the Phocian (Greek) port will be broken, leading to the capture of a city and repeated enemy assaults—signifying a breaking of a long‑standing barrier and renewed warfare.

french: Du règne Anglois l’indigne desséché,
Le conseiller par ire mis à feu:
Ses adhérents iront si bas tracer,
Que le bâtard sera demi reçu.
english: From the English reign the unworthy dried out,
The counsellor by anger set on fire:
His followers will go so low to trace,
That the bastard will be half‑received.
80.interpretation: A corrupt English ruler will be purged; an angry advisor will be executed. His lowly supporters will fall so far that an illegitimate heir will only be partially accepted—predicting a dynastic crisis in England.

french: Le grand criard sans honte audacieux,
Sera élu gouverneur de l’armée:
La hardiesse de son contentieux,
Le pont rompu, cité de peur pasmée:
english: The great boastful shout without shame, daring,
Will be elected governor of the army:
The boldness of his dispute,
The broken bridge, city of terrified awe.
81.interpretation: A reckless, arrogant commander will be made army governor. His bold quarrels will cause a bridge to collapse, leaving a city in stunned terror—signalling a reckless military leader whose actions bring disaster.

french: Freins, Antibol, villes au tour de Nice,
Seront vastées fer, par mer & par terre:
Les sauterelles terre & mer vent propice,
Princes, morts, troussés, pillés sans loi de guerre.
english: Brakes, Antibol, cities around Nice,
Will be stripped of iron, by sea and by land:
The locusts of earth & sea with favorable wind,
Princes, dead, beaten, pillaged without war law.
82.interpretation: The area around Nice will be stripped of metal resources by both sea and land forces. Swarms (locusts) aided by favorable winds will devastate, killing nobles and looting without any wartime code—indicating total devastation of the Riviera region.

french: Les longs cheueux de la Gaule Celtique
Accompagnés d’étranges nations,
Mètront captif la gent Aquitanique,
Pour succomber à internitions.
english: The long chiefs of Celtic Gaul
Accompanied by strange nations,
Will capture the Aquitanian people,
To succumb to their incursions.
83.interpretation: Celtic Gaulish leaders, allied with foreign peoples, will capture Aquitaine, allowing invasions to succeed—foretelling a coordinated conquest of southwestern France.

french: La grand cité sera bien désolée
Des habitants vn seul ny demeurra:
Mur, sexe, temple, & vierge violée
Par fer, feu, peste, canon peuple mourra.
english: The great city will be truly deserted,
Only one inhabitant will remain:
Wall, sex, temple, & virgin violated
By iron, fire, plague, cannon the people will die.
84.interpretation: A major metropolis will become a ghost town, with only a solitary survivor. Its walls, temples and morals will be shattered by war, disease and artillery—signalling total destruction of a major urban centre.

french: La cité prinse par tromperie & fraude,
Par le moyen d’vn beau jeune attrapé:
L’assaut donné Roubine près de l’AVDE
Lui et tous morts pour avoir bien trompé.
english: The captured city by trickery and fraud,
Through the means of a beautiful young man caught:
The assault given to Roubine near the AVDE
He and all dead for having deceived well.
85.interpretation: A city will be taken through deceit involving a charming young man. An assault on a place called Roubine (perhaps Rouen) will result in many deaths due to the deception—foretelling a betrayal that leads to a failed siege.

french: Le chef d’Ausonne aux Hespagnes ira
Par mer fera arrest dans Marseille:
Auant sa mort vn long temps languira:
Après sa mort lon verra grand merueille.
english: The chief of Auvers will go to the Spanish,
By sea will arrest within Marseille:
Before his death a long time will languish:
After his death long will see great marvel.
86.interpretation: A leader from Auvers will travel to Spain, be captured at sea near Marseille, then endure a long decline before dying. After his death, an extraordinary event will be witnessed—suggesting a dramatic maritime capture and subsequent wonder.

french: Classe Gauloyse n’approches de Corseigne
Moins de sardaigne, tu t’en repentiras
Trestous morts frustrés de laide Grogne:
Sang nagera: captif ne me croyras.
english: Gaulish class does not approach Corsica
Less than Sardinia, you will regret it
Many dead frustrated by useless groan:
Blood will flow: captive you will not believe.
87.interpretation: Gaulish forces will avoid Corsica, regretting missing Sardinia. Numerous dead will be left in frustration, and blood will run, surprising captives—signalling missed opportunities and heavy casualties in Mediterranean operations.

french: De Barcelonne par mer si grand armée,
Toute Marseille de frayeur tremblera:
Isles saisies de mer aide fermée,
Ton traître en terre nagera.
english: From Barcelona by sea such a great army,
All of Marseille will tremble with fear:
Islands seized, the sea's aid closed,
Your traitor on land will drown.
88.interpretation: A massive fleet from Barcelona will threaten Marseille, causing terror. Islands will be captured, aid cut off, and a betrayer on land will be drowned—foretelling a major naval assault on southern France.

french: En ce temps la sera frustré Cypres
De son secours, de ceux de mer Egée:
Vieux trucidés: mais par masles & lyphres
Seduict leur roy, royne plus outragée.
english: At this time the Cypress will be frustrated
Of its help, of those of the Aegean sea:
Old slain: but by hooks & scales
They seduced their king, more outraged.
89.interpretation: The Cypress (symbolic of Greece) will be thwarted despite Aegean support. Elderly leaders fall due to treachery (hooks & scales), leading to further royal outrage—signifying a failed rescue of a Greek state.

french: Le grand Satyre & Tigre de Hyrcanie,
Don présente à ceux de l’Ocean:
Le chef de classe istra de Carmanie
Qui prendra terre au Tyrren Phocean.
english: The great Satyr & Tiger of Hyrcania,
Gift given to those of the Ocean:
The chief of the class of Caramanian explorers
Who will take land at the Tyrrhenian Phocean.
90.interpretation: Mythic figures (Satyr, Tiger) will be offered to maritime peoples. A Caramanian leader will seize territory on the Tyrrhenian Sea—foretelling a symbolic gifting of power leading to conquest on Italy’s western coast.

french: L’arbre qu’auoit par long temps mort seché,
Dans vne nuit viendra a reuerdir:
Cron.roy malade, prince pied estaché
Craint d’ennemis fera voile bondir.
english: The tree that had long been dead dried,
In one night will come to revive:
Crowned king ill, prince foot cut
Fearful of enemies will make the veil jump.
91.interpretation: A dead tree will spring to life overnight, symbolising a resurrected power. A sick king and injured prince will react to enemy threats, raising the curtain (war) quickly—suggesting a sudden revival of a dynasty amidst crises.

french: Le monde proche du dernier période,
Saturne encore tard sera de retour:
Translat empire devers nation Brodde:
L’œil arraché à Narbon par Autour.
english: The world near the last period,
Saturn still late will return:
Translate empire towards the nation Brodden:
The eye ripped from Narbonne by Around.
92.interpretation: In the final era, Saturn (a symbol of time) will return, moving the empire toward a nation called Brodden. Narbonne will lose its "eye" (perhaps a key figure) due to surrounding forces—indicating a late‑stage shift of power and loss of a crucial city.

french: Dans Auignon tout le chef de l’empire
Fera arrest pour Paris désolé:
Tricast tiendra l’Annibalique ire:
Lyon par change sera mal consolé.
english: In Auvergne the whole head of the empire
Will be detained for a desolate Paris:
Tricast will hold the Annibalic anger:
Lyon by change will be badly consoled.
93.interpretation: The imperial leader in Auvergne will be detained, implicating Paris. A force called Tricast will kindle a Hannibal‑like wrath, while Lyon suffers from a disappointing change—foretelling political turmoil affecting the French capital and city‑states.

french: De cinq cent ans plus compte lon tiendra
Celuy qu’estoit l’ornement de son temps:
Puis à vn coup grande clarté donrra
Que par ce siècle les rendra très contents.
english: From five hundred years more we will hold
The one who was the ornament of his time:
Then in one blow great light will give
That by this century will make them very happy.
94.interpretation: Over the next five centuries, a celebrated figure will be remembered; a sudden brilliance will appear, bringing great happiness to the age—suggesting a lasting legacy culminating in a transformative revelation.

french: La loy Moricque on verra defaillir:
Après vne autre beaucoup plus seductiue,
Boristhenes premier viendra faillir:
Pardons & langue vne plus attractive.
english: The Moorish law will be seen to fail:
After another much more seductive,
Boristhenes the first will come to fail:
Pardons and language more attractive.
95.interpretation: Moorish legal customs will collapse, replaced by a more alluring system. The first Boristhenes (a ruler) will also fail, leading to new, appealing pardons and language—signalling cultural and legal replacement of Moorish influence.

french: Chef de FOVSSAN aura gorge couper
Par le ducteur du limier & leurier:
Le fait patré par ceux du mont TARPEE
Saturne en Leo XIII. de Feurier.
english: Chief of FOVSSAN will have his throat cut
By the driver of the harvester and plow:
The deed done by those of Mount Tarpee
Saturn in Leo XIII of February.
96.interpretation: The leader of FOVSSAN will be beheaded by agricultural workers. The killing will be carried out by people from Mount Tarpee, coinciding with a Saturn‑in‑Leo event in February—a violent political purge tied to astrological timing.

french: Nouvelle loi terre neufue occuper
Vers la Syrie, Iudée, & Palestine:
Le grand empire barbare corruer,
Auant que Phebés son siècle détermine.
english: New law, new earth to occupy
Towards Syria, Judea, & Palestine:
The great barbarian empire will collapse,
Before Phebé's century is determined.
97.interpretation: A fresh decree will claim new lands in the Levant. The vast barbarian empire will fall before the era of Phebé (a future period) is set—suggesting territorial expansion and the downfall of an empire in the Near East.

french: Deus royals frères si fort guerroyeront [Deux]
Qu’entre eux sera la guerre si mortelle,
Qu’vn chacun places fortes occuperont:
De règne & vie sera leur grand querelle.
english: Divine royal brothers will war so fiercely [Two],
Between them there will be a deadly war,
Each will occupy strong places:
From reign and life will be their great dispute.
98.interpretation: Two royal brothers will engage in a brutal civil war, each seizing fortified positions, fighting over authority and survival—foretelling a fratricidal conflict for the throne.

french: Aux champs herbeux d’Alein & du Varneigne,
Du mont Lebron proche de la Durance,
Camp de deux pars conflict sera sy aigre:
Mesopotamie defallira en la France.
english: In the grassy fields of Alen and Varneigne,
From the Lebron mountain near the Durance,
A camp of two parts conflict will be bitter:
Mesopotamia will fall into France.
99.interpretation: Battles in the fields near Alen and Varneigne, close to the Durance River, will be bitter between two forces. The conflict will bring Mesopotamian influence into France—signalling a western front where eastern powers intrude.

french: Entre Gaulois le dernier honoré,
D’homme ennemi sera victorieux:
Force & terroir en moment exploré,
D’un coup de trait quand mourra l’ennuyeux.
english: Among Gauls the last honoured,
From enemy man will be victorious:
Strength and land in a moment explored,
With a single thrust when the boring one dies.
100.interpretation: The final celebrated Gaul will defeat his enemy. Strength and territory will be seized swiftly, ending with a single decisive strike that kills the lingering opponent—foretelling a final, decisive victory for the Gauls.
